verses.flow: That which was from the beginning, that which we have heard, that which we have seen with our eyes, that which we beheld and our hands handled, concerning the Word of life—
verses.note: GREEK: The neuter relative pronoun "ho" (ὅ, "that which") is used rather than the masculine "hos," emphasizing not merely the person of Christ but the fullness of the revelation experienced. "Apsēlaphēsan" (ἐψηλάφησαν, Strong's G5584) means to touch, feel, or grope after—a term of physical, tactile contact, stressing incarnational reality against early docetic denials. The perfect tenses "akēkoamen" (we have heard) and "heōrakamen" (we have seen) indicate abiding results of past experience. THEOLOGICAL: "The Word of life" (tou logou tēs zōēs) echoes John 1:1-4, identifying the pre-existent Logos as the source and content of life itself. TYPOLOGY: The "from the beginning" (ap' archēs) parallels Genesis 1:1 (LXX: en archē) and John 1:1, placing Christ at the origin of all things.

verses.flow: and the life was manifested, and we have seen and we bear witness and we declare to you the life eternal, which was with the Father and was manifested to us—
verses.note: GREEK: "Ephanerōthē" (ἐφανερώθη, Strong's G5319) is aorist passive of "phaneroō"—to make visible, to manifest—indicating a decisive divine act of revelation. The life was not discovered but disclosed by God's initiative. THEOLOGICAL: "Which was with the Father" (hētis ēn pros ton patera) uses "pros" with the accusative, denoting face-to-face relational presence—the identical construction found in John 1:1 (ho logos ēn pros ton theon). This affirms the eternal pre-existence of the Son in intimate communion with the Father prior to the incarnation.

verses.flow: that which we have seen and heard we declare also to you, so that you also may have fellowship with us; and indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son, Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Koinōnia" (κοινωνία, Strong's G2842) denotes shared participation, a deep mutual communion—not mere social association. The fellowship believers enter is nothing less than participation in the communion that exists between the Father and the Son. STRUCTURE: Verse 3 completes the suspended sentence begun in verse 1, finally supplying the main verb "apangellomen" (we declare), creating a rhetorically powerful crescendo from sensory testimony to proclamation to divine fellowship.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And these things we write, so that our joy may be made complete.
And this is the message which we have heard from him and declare to you, that God is light, and darkness in him there is none at all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "God is light" (ho theos phōs estin) is an ontological declaration—not merely that God gives light but that he is light in his very essence. "Skotia en autō ouk estin oudemia"—the double negation (ouk...oudemia) is emphatic in Greek, producing an absolute exclusion: no darkness whatsoever exists in him. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Light as a divine attribute pervades the heavenly throne-room imagery of Scripture (Ps 104:2; Dan 7:9-10; Rev 21:23). God's nature as pure light establishes the moral foundation for the ethical exhortations that follow in verses 6-10.

verses.flow: If we say that we have fellowship with him and walk in the darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth.
But if we walk in the light, as he himself is in the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katharizei" (καθαρίζει, Strong's G2511) is present active indicative—denoting continuous, ongoing cleansing, not a single past event. TYPOLOGY: The blood that cleanses echoes the Levitical sacrificial system (Lev 16:15-19) and the Passover lamb (Exod 12:13), both fulfilled in Christ. The phrase "from all sin" (apo pasēs hamartias) indicates comprehensive atonement with no category of transgression excluded. THEOLOGICAL: Walking in the light does not mean sinless perfection but living in transparency before God, where the blood of Jesus continually purifies the believer.

verses.flow: If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
If we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous, so that he may forgive us our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
verses.note: GREEK: "Homologōmen" (ὁμολογῶμεν, Strong's G3670) means literally "to say the same thing"—to agree with God's assessment of our sin. THEOLOGICAL: God's forgiveness is grounded in two divine attributes: "pistos" (faithful)—he is bound by his covenant promises—and "dikaios" (righteous)—forgiveness is not a suspension of justice but its satisfaction through the atoning work of Christ (Rom 3:25-26). "Aphē" (ἀφῇ, aorist subjunctive of aphiēmi) means to send away or release, picturing sin as a debt cancelled. "Adikia" (unrighteousness) broadens the scope beyond individual acts to encompass all moral corruption.

verses.flow: If we say that we have not sinned, we make him a liar, and his word is not in us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pseustēn poioumen auton"—"we make him a liar"—is among the gravest accusations possible, for it assaults the very character of God who has testified to human sinfulness throughout Scripture (Gen 6:5; Ps 14:3; Rom 3:23). GREEK: "Ho logos autou" (his word) may refer both to the proclaimed gospel message and to Christ himself as the living Word (cf. v. 1; John 1:1), deepening the severity of the denial. The perfect tense "hēmartēkamen" (we have sinned) denotes a settled state—denying not just occasional failings but the abiding reality of human sinfulness.

verses.flow: My little children, these things I write to you so that you may not sin. And if anyone should sin, we have an Advocate with the Father — Jesus Christ, the Righteous One.
verses.note: GREEK: "Paraklētos" (παράκλητος, Strong's G3875) means "one called alongside" — an advocate or intercessor. The same term is used by John for the Holy Spirit (John 14:16, 26; 15:26; 16:7). Here it designates Christ's ongoing intercessory role before the Father. "Dikaios" (δίκαιος) — "Righteous One" — stands in attributive position emphasizing Christ's qualification to advocate on behalf of sinners.

verses.flow: And He Himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only, but also for the whole world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Hilasmos" (ἱλασμός, Strong's G2434) denotes propitiation — the turning away of divine wrath through an atoning sacrifice. This is not mere expiation (removal of sin) but satisfaction of God's righteous justice. The scope is universal in provision ("the whole world") though applied through faith. This echoes the Day of Atonement typology where the high priest made atonement for all Israel (Lev 16:15-17). TYPOLOGY: Christ fulfills the role of both the sacrifice and the mercy seat (hilastērion, Rom 3:25), the place where God's wrath is propitiated.

verses.flow: And by this we know that we have come to know Him: if we keep His commandments.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one saying, "I have come to know Him," and not keeping His commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in this one.
But whoever keeps His word, truly in this one the love of God has been perfected. By this we know that we are in Him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Teteleiotai" (τετελείωται) is a perfect passive indicative of "teleioō" (Strong's G5048), meaning "has been brought to completion" or "has been perfected." The perfect tense indicates a completed action with ongoing results. The love of God reaches its intended goal and completion in the one who keeps His word. THEOLOGICAL: This is not human perfection but the maturation and fulfillment of God's covenant love in the obedient believer.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one saying he abides in Him ought himself also to walk even as that One walked.
Beloved, I write no new commandment to you, but an old commandment which you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word which you heard.
Again, a new commandment I write to you, which is true in Him and in you, because the darkness is passing away and the true light already shines.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of a commandment both old and new reflects the inaugurated eschatology of the New Covenant. The love command was given "from the beginning" (Lev 19:18; Deut 6:5), yet is made new in Christ's fulfillment and the indwelling Spirit (John 13:34). The darkness "passing away" (paragetai, present tense) indicates the already-not-yet nature of the present age.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one saying he is in the light and hating his brother is in the darkness until now.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one loving his brother abides in the light, and there is no cause for stumbling in him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one hating his brother is in the darkness and walks in the darkness, and does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.
I write to you, little children, because your sins have been forgiven you on account of His name.
I write to you, fathers, because you have known the One from the beginning. I write to you, young men, because you have overcome the evil one.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: John addresses three groups — little children (teknia/paidia), fathers (pateres), and young men (neaniskoi) — in a carefully structured double address (vv. 12-14). This may reflect stages of spiritual maturity within the community. "The One from the beginning" (ton ap' archēs) echoes the prologue of the Gospel of John (John 1:1) and 1 John 1:1, identifying Christ with the eternal Word.

verses.flow: I have written to you, little children, because you have known the Father. I have written to you, fathers, because you have known the One from the beginning. I have written to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God abides in you, and you have overcome the evil one.
Do not love the world, nor the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.
For all that is in the world — the desire of the flesh, and the desire of the eyes, and the boastful pride of life — is not of the Father, but is of the world.
verses.note: GREEK: "Epithymia" (ἐπιθυμία, Strong's G1939) denotes strong desire or craving, not merely temptation. "Alazoneia" (ἀλαζονεία, Strong's G212) refers to arrogant boasting or pretentious pride, originally describing a wandering quack or braggart. "Bios" (βίος) here means livelihood or material life, distinct from "zōē" (spiritual/eternal life). TYPOLOGY: This threefold pattern of worldly temptation echoes the temptation of Eve in the Garden — good for food, a delight to the eyes, desirable to make one wise (Gen 3:6) — and Christ's threefold temptation in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the world is passing away, and its desire; but the one doing the will of God abides unto the age.
Little children, it is the last hour; and just as you heard that antichrist is coming, even now many antichrists have arisen, from which we know that it is the last hour.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Antichristos" (ἀντίχριστος, Strong's G500) appears only in 1-2 John in the NT. The prefix "anti-" can mean both "against" and "in place of" Christ. John identifies a singular eschatological antichrist figure yet to come, while noting that many "antichrists" — those embodying the spirit of opposition to Christ — have already arisen. This reflects Second Temple Jewish expectations of an end-time adversary (cf. Dan 7:25; 2 Thess 2:3-4). THEOLOGICAL: The presence of multiple antichrists serves as evidence that the "last hour" (eschatē hōra) has arrived with Christ's first advent.

verses.flow: They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have remained with us. But it was so that they might be made manifest, that not all are of us.
And you have an anointing from the Holy One, and you all know.
verses.note: GREEK: "Chrisma" (χρῖσμα, Strong's G5545) means "anointing" and is cognate with "Christos" (Anointed One). This refers to the Holy Spirit given to believers, echoing the anointing of priests and kings in the OT (Exod 30:22-33; 1 Sam 16:13). Believers share in the anointing of Christ Himself. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "Holy One" (tou hagiou) from whom the anointing comes likely refers to Christ, who is called the Holy One of God (Mark 1:24; John 6:69), though the Father as source is also possible.

verses.flow: I have not written to you because you do not know the truth, but because you know it, and because every lie is not of the truth.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Who is the liar, if not the one denying that Jesus is the Christ? This one is the antichrist — the one denying the Father and the Son.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John defines the essential mark of the antichrist spirit: denial that Jesus is the Christ (the Anointed One, the Messiah). This directly targets early proto-Gnostic and Cerinthian teachings that separated the divine Christ from the human Jesus. To deny the Son is simultaneously to deny the Father — the Father-Son relationship is indivisible in Johannine theology (John 10:30; 14:9-11).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Everyone who denies the Son does not have the Father either. The one confessing the Son has the Father also.
As for you, let what you heard from the beginning abide in you. If what you heard from the beginning abides in you, you also will abide in the Son and in the Father.
And this is the promise which He Himself promised to us: the life eternal.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: These things I have written to you concerning those who are leading you astray.

And as for you, the anointing which you received from Him abides in you, and you have no need that anyone should teach you; but as His anointing teaches you concerning all things, and is true and is not a lie, and just as it taught you — abide in Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "chrisma" (anointing) here functions as the internal teacher — the indwelling Holy Spirit who guides believers into all truth (John 16:13). John does not negate the role of human teachers but affirms that the Spirit's testimony provides the ultimate criterion for discerning truth from error. The anointing is described as "true" (alēthes) and "not a lie" (ouk estin pseudos), standing in direct contrast to the false teachers of v. 26.

verses.flow: And now, little children, abide in Him, so that when He is made manifest, we may have boldness and not be put to shame before Him at His coming.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: If you know that He is righteous, you know that everyone who practices righteousness has been begotten of Him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Gegennētai" (γεγέννηται) is perfect passive of "gennaō" (Strong's G1080), meaning "has been begotten" or "has been born." The perfect tense indicates an abiding state of divine origin. The passive voice points to God as the agent of this spiritual birth. THEOLOGICAL: Practicing righteousness is not the cause but the evidence of divine begetting — the one born of God bears the family likeness of the Righteous One. This prepares for the major theme of 1 John 3:1-10.

verses.flow: Behold what manner of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God — and we are. On account of this the world does not know us, because it did not know Him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Behold" (ἴδετε, idete) is an aorist imperative of horaō — a command to gaze with astonishment. "What manner of" (ποταπήν, potapēn) expresses quality rather than quantity, conveying wonder at the foreign or extraordinary nature of this love. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "and we are" (καὶ ἐσμέν) is a parenthetical affirmation — not merely a title but an ontological reality. The world's failure to recognize believers mirrors its failure to recognize Christ Himself (John 1:10-11).

verses.flow: Beloved, now we are children of God, and it has not yet been made manifest what we shall be. We know that when He is made manifest, we shall be like Him, because we shall see Him just as He is.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "not yet made manifest" (οὔπω ἐφανερώθη) points to an eschatological transformation still awaited. The promise of being "like Him" (ὅμοιοι αὐτῷ) because "we shall see Him just as He is" ties vision of God to glorification — the beatific vision transforms the beholder (2 Cor 3:18). GREEK: φανερωθῇ (phanerōthē) — the same verb used of Christ's incarnational appearing — now refers to His return or final revelation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And everyone having this hope upon Him purifies himself, just as that One is pure.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Everyone who practices sin also practices lawlessness, and sin is lawlessness.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνομία (anomia, Strong's G458) means "lawlessness" — not mere transgression but active rebellion against the Torah/divine order. John equates sin (ἁμαρτία) with lawlessness, defining sin not as weakness but as opposition to God's established rule. THEOLOGICAL: This identification counters any antinomian tendency that would separate faith from obedience to divine commandment.

verses.flow: And you know that that One was made manifest in order that He might take away sins, and sin is not in Him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Everyone who abides in Him does not sin. Everyone who sins has not seen Him nor has known Him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Little children, let no one lead you astray. The one who practices righteousness is righteous, just as that One is righteous.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one who practices sin is of the devil, because from the beginning the devil sins. For this purpose the Son of God was made manifest, that He might destroy the works of the devil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "From the beginning" (ἀπ' ἀρχῆς) echoes Genesis 3 and the primordial rebellion. The Son of God was made manifest (ἐφανερώθη) — the incarnation has a specific cosmic-warfare purpose: to destroy (λύσῃ, lysē — lit. "loose, dissolve, undo") the works of the devil. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This reflects the divine council framework in which the Son enters the domain of rebellious spiritual powers to dismantle their authority (Col 2:15; Heb 2:14). TYPOLOGY: Christ as the seed of the woman (Gen 3:15) crushing the serpent's works finds its apostolic interpretation here.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Everyone who has been begotten of God does not practice sin, because His seed abides in him, and he is not able to sin, because he has been begotten of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Seed" (σπέρμα, sperma, Strong's G4690) refers to divine generative principle — the life-giving reality implanted by God in the believer. "Has been begotten" (γεγεννημένος/γεγέννηται) is perfect tense, indicating a completed action with ongoing results. THEOLOGICAL: The impossibility of sinning for the one begotten of God describes the fundamental incompatibility between the divine nature received and the practice of habitual sin — not sinless perfection but a transformed orientation of life.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: In this the children of God and the children of the devil are manifest: everyone who does not practice righteousness is not of God, and the one who does not love his brother.
For this is the message which you heard from the beginning, that we should love one another;
not as Cain was of the evil one and slew his brother. And for what cause did he slay him? Because his works were evil, but those of his brother were righteous.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Cain (Κάϊν) serves as the archetype of the one "of the evil one" (ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ) — his murder of Abel prefigures the world's hatred of the righteous. The righteous suffer precisely because their works expose the evil of the wicked (Gen 4:1-8). HISTORICAL: In Second Temple Jewish tradition (e.g., Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on Gen 4), Cain was associated with Satanic or demonic paternity, a tradition John appears to affirm.

verses.flow: Do not marvel, brothers, if the world hates you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: We know that we have passed over out of death into life, because we love the brothers. The one who does not love abides in death.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that every murderer does not have eternal life abiding in him.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνθρωποκτόνος (anthrōpoktónos, Strong's G443) — "murderer," literally "man-slayer." This rare word appears only here and in John 8:44, where Jesus calls the devil a "murderer from the beginning." John thus links hatred of a brother to the character of the devil himself. THEOLOGICAL: The absence of eternal life in the murderer is not merely future judgment but present spiritual reality — hatred is the inward reality of which murder is the outward expression.

verses.flow: In this we have known love: that that One laid down His life on behalf of us; and we ought to lay down our lives on behalf of the brothers.
verses.note: GREEK: "Laid down His life" (τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἔθηκεν) echoes Jesus' own words in John 10:11, 15, 17-18, using the same verb τίθημι. ψυχή here denotes the whole life given in sacrificial death. THEOLOGICAL: The cross is the definitive revelation of what love (ἀγάπη) is — not sentiment but self-sacrifice. The "ought" (ὀφείλομεν) places upon believers a binding obligation to imitate this pattern of costly love.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But whoever has the livelihood of the world and beholds his brother having need and shuts his bowels of compassion from him, how does the love of God abide in him?
verses.note: GREEK: σπλάγχνα (splanchna, Strong's G4698) — "bowels," the seat of deep compassion in ancient understanding (comparable to "heart" in modern usage). "Shuts" (κλείσῃ) is an aorist subjunctive indicating a deliberate act of closing off compassion. THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "how does the love of God abide in him?" expects the answer: it does not. Love of God is verified or falsified by material generosity toward the needy brother.

verses.flow: Little children, let us not love in word nor in tongue, but in deed and in truth.
And in this we shall know that we are of the truth, and before Him we shall assure our heart,
that if our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart and knows all things.
Beloved, if our heart does not condemn us, we have boldness toward God,
and whatever we ask, we receive from Him, because we keep His commandments and do the things pleasing before Him.
And this is His commandment: that we should believe in the name of His Son Jesus Christ, and should love one another, just as He gave commandment to us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The singular "commandment" (ἐντολή) encompasses two dimensions: faith and love — believing in the name of Jesus Christ and loving one another. These are not separable obligations but one unified command. GREEK: "Believe in the name" (πιστεύσωμεν τῷ ὀνόματι) uses the dative, indicating trust directed toward and resting upon the person designated by the name. The full title "His Son Jesus Christ" emphasizes divine sonship, historical identity, and messianic office together.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the one who keeps His commandments abides in Him, and He in him. And in this we know that He abides in us: from the Spirit whom He gave to us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Mutual abiding — the believer in God and God in the believer — is the culminating theme. The assurance of this indwelling comes "from the Spirit whom He gave to us" (ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος οὗ ἡμῖν ἔδωκεν). This is the first explicit mention of the Spirit in 1 John, preparing for the extended discussion of discerning spirits in chapter 4. GREEK: πνεύματος (pneumatos, Strong's G4151) — Spirit, here the Holy Spirit as the divine agent who mediates and confirms the mutual indwelling of God and believer.

verses.flow: Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits, whether they are from God, because many false prophets have gone out into the world.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb δοκιμάζετε (dokimazete, Strong's G1381) means "to test, examine, prove by trial," used of metals tested by fire. The present imperative indicates an ongoing discipline of discernment. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The plural πνεύματα (pneumata) acknowledges a real spiritual realm of competing spirits—both from God and from the realm of rebellious spiritual beings—requiring believers to exercise discernment (cf. Deut 13:1-5; 1 Cor 12:10).

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: By this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ having come in the flesh is from God,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The confession test is specifically christological: ὁμολογεῖ Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ἐν σαρκὶ ἐληλυθότα—"confesses Jesus Christ having come in the flesh." The perfect participle ἐληλυθότα (elēlythota) indicates a completed action with ongoing results: the incarnation is a permanent reality. HISTORICAL: This targets proto-Gnostic and Docetic teachers who denied the true materiality of Christ's body, a serious threat in late first-century Asia Minor.

verses.flow: and every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God; and this is the spirit of the antichrist, which you have heard that it is coming, and now it is in the world already.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term ἀντίχριστος (antichristos, Strong's G500) appears only in 1 John and 2 John in the NT. John identifies the spirit of antichrist not as a future figure alone but as an already-present reality operative through false teachers who deny the incarnation (cf. 1 John 2:18, 22; 2 John 7).

verses.flow: You are from God, little children, and you have overcome them, because greater is the One who is in you than the one who is in the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The perfect tense νενικήκατε (nenikēkate, "you have overcome") indicates a decisive and abiding victory. The comparative μείζων (meizōn, "greater") establishes a contrast between the Holy Spirit indwelling believers and the spiritual power animating the world system (cf. John 12:31; 16:11). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse presupposes a cosmic conflict between God and hostile spiritual powers, with believers participating in Christ's victory over the rulers of this age.

verses.flow: They are from the world; on account of this they speak from the world, and the world hears them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: We are from God. The one knowing God hears us; the one who is not from God does not hear us. From this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Beloved, let us love one another, because love is from God, and everyone who loves has been born from God and knows God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one who does not love has not known God, because God is love.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration ὁ θεὸς ἀγάπη ἐστίν ("God is love") is one of the most profound ontological statements in Scripture. Note that John does not say "love is God" but "God is love"—love is an essential attribute of God's nature, not a definition that reduces God to an abstract concept. The aorist ἔγνω (egnō) suggests the one not loving never truly came to know God at all.

verses.flow: In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God has sent His only-begotten Son into the world, so that we might live through Him.
verses.note: GREEK: The term μονογενῆ (monogenē, Strong's G3439) means "only-begotten" or "one-of-a-kind, unique." It denotes the singular, unrepeatable relationship of the Son to the Father. TYPOLOGY: The sending of the only-begotten Son echoes Abraham's offering of Isaac (Gen 22:2, 12, 16 LXX where μονογενής is used), establishing a typological link between the Aqedah and God's sacrificial gift (cf. John 3:16).

verses.flow: In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that He loved us and sent His Son as a propitiation concerning our sins.
verses.note: GREEK: The word ἱλασμόν (hilasmon, Strong's G2434) means "propitiation"—an offering that turns away divine wrath by satisfying the demands of justice. It appears in the NT only here and in 1 John 2:2. THEOLOGICAL: John emphasizes the initiative of divine love: the propitiation originates not in human effort toward God but in God's own self-giving act of sending His Son. Love and wrath are not contradictory; the cross satisfies both (cf. Rom 3:25).

verses.flow: Beloved, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: No one has ever beheld God. If we love one another, God abides in us, and His love has been perfected in us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: θεὸν οὐδεὶς πώποτε τεθέαται ("No one has ever beheld God") echoes John 1:18. The perfect tense τεθέαται emphasizes that no human has ever gazed upon God in His unveiled essence. Yet God's invisible presence becomes tangibly manifest through mutual love among believers—His love is τετελειωμένη (teteleiōmenē, "perfected, brought to its intended completion") in the community.

verses.flow: By this we know that we abide in Him and He in us, because He has given to us from His Spirit.
And we have beheld and we bear witness that the Father has sent the Son as Savior of the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Father's sending (ἀπέσταλκεν, apostalken, perfect tense—sent with abiding result) of the Son as σωτῆρα τοῦ κόσμου ("Savior of the world") declares the universal scope of Christ's saving mission. The title σωτήρ was used in the imperial cult for Caesar; John's application to Christ is a direct counter-claim to Roman imperial theology (cf. John 4:42).

verses.flow: Whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God abides in him, and he in God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And we have known and have believed the love that God has in us. God is love, and the one abiding in love abides in God, and God abides in him.
In this love has been perfected with us, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment, because just as that One is, so also are we in this world.
verses.note: GREEK: παρρησίαν (parrēsian, Strong's G3954) denotes "boldness, confidence, freedom of speech"—originally a political term for the citizen's right to speak freely in the assembly. Here it describes the believer's confidence before the divine Judge. THEOLOGICAL: The basis for this boldness is ontological conformity: καθὼς ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν καὶ ἡμεῖς ἐσμεν—"just as that One is, so also are we." Present-tense identification with Christ in this world becomes the ground of fearlessness on the day of κρίσεως (kriseōs, judgment).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Fear is not in love, but perfect love casts out fear, because fear has punishment; and the one who fears has not been perfected in love.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb βάλλει (ballei, "casts out") is vivid and forceful—the same verb used for casting out demons (Mark 1:34). Perfect love does not merely reduce fear; it violently expels it. The term κόλασιν (kolasin, Strong's G2851) means "punishment" or "torment," indicating that fear itself carries a punitive, tormenting quality incompatible with the perfection of love.

verses.flow: We love, because He first loved us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The priority of divine love is the theological foundation of the entire chapter. The pronoun αὐτός (autos, "He") is emphatic, and πρῶτος (prōtos, "first") establishes absolute temporal and causal priority: God's love is not a response to ours but its origin and cause (cf. Rom 5:8; Eph 2:4-5).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: If anyone says, "I love God," and hates his brother, he is a liar; for the one who does not love his brother whom he has seen, is not able to love God whom he has not seen.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And this commandment we have from Him: that the one who loves God should love also his brother.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been begotten of God, and everyone who loves the One having begotten loves also the one having been begotten of Him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Begotten" (γεγέννηται, gegennētai, perfect passive of gennaō, Strong's G1080) indicates a completed divine act with ongoing results. The perfect tense emphasizes the abiding state of one who has been born of God. THEOLOGICAL: John ties together faith in Jesus as the Christ (Messiah) with divine begetting — belief is both evidence and fruit of regeneration. The familial logic is striking: to love the Father who begets is inseparable from loving those begotten by Him.

verses.flow: By this we know that we love the children of God: when we love God and do His commandments.
For this is the love of God, that we keep His commandments; and His commandments are not burdensome.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For everything that has been begotten of God overcomes the world; and this is the victory that has overcome the world — our faith.
verses.note: GREEK: "Overcomes" (νικᾷ, nika, Strong's G3528) is present tense, indicating ongoing victory, while "has overcome" (νικήσασα, nikēsasa) is aorist participle — a decisive, accomplished victory. THEOLOGICAL: The victory over the world-system (kosmos) is attributed not to human effort but to the new-birth origin ("begotten of God") and its instrument, faith. This echoes Jesus' declaration in John 16:33: "I have overcome the world."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And who is the one overcoming the world, if not the one believing that Jesus is the Son of God?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: This is the One having come through water and blood — Jesus Christ — not by the water only, but by the water and by the blood. And the Spirit is the One bearing witness, because the Spirit is the truth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Water and blood" most likely refers to the baptism and crucifixion of Jesus Christ, the bookends of His public ministry. John counters early docetic or Cerinthian heresy that denied Christ came in true flesh or that the divine Christ departed before the cross. GREEK: The emphatic repetition — "not by the water only, but by the water and by the blood" — uses the Greek article (τῷ) to make each element specific and definite. The Spirit (τὸ πνεῦμα, to pneuma) serves as the ongoing divine witness to these historical realities.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For there are three that bear witness:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The longer reading known as the Comma Johanneum (adding "in heaven: the Father, the Word, and the Holy Spirit, and these three are one") found in some Latin manuscripts is absent from all early Greek manuscripts and is widely regarded as a later interpolation. The critical Greek text preserves only the shorter reading presented here.

verses.flow: the Spirit and the water and the blood, and the three are unto the one.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The threefold witness of Spirit, water, and blood corresponds to the requirements of Deuteronomy 19:15 that testimony be established by two or three witnesses. The three converge "unto the one" (εἰς τὸ ἕν) — they agree in a single, unified testimony concerning the identity and saving work of Jesus Christ.

verses.flow: If the witness of men we receive, the witness of God is greater; for this is the witness of God, that He has borne witness concerning His Son.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one believing in the Son of God has the witness in himself. The one not believing God has made Him a liar, because he has not believed in the witness which God has witnessed concerning His Son.
And this is the witness: that God gave to us eternal life, and this life is in His Son.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one having the Son has the life; the one not having the Son of God does not have the life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents the most distilled summary of Johannine soteriology: eternal life is exclusively mediated through the Son. The starkly parallel construction — having the Son equals having life; not having the Son equals not having life — allows no middle ground. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the pattern of life being found only "in" God's appointed means, as the tree of life was in Eden (Gen 2:9) and as Israel's life was in covenant obedience (Deut 30:19-20).

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: These things I wrote to you, that you may know that you have eternal life — to the ones believing in the name of the Son of God.
And this is the confidence which we have toward Him, that if anything we ask according to His will, He hears us.
And if we know that He hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we have the requests which we have asked from Him.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: If anyone sees his brother sinning a sin not unto death, he shall ask, and He will give to him life — to those sinning not unto death. There is a sin unto death; I do not say that one should make request concerning that.
All unrighteousness is sin, and there is sin not unto death.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: We know that everyone having been begotten of God does not sin, but the One begotten of God keeps him, and the evil one does not touch him.
verses.note: GREEK: There is a crucial distinction between "the one having been begotten" (ὁ γεγεννημένος, ho gegennēmenos, referring to the believer) and "the One begotten" (ὁ γεννηθείς, ho gennētheis, aorist passive, referring to Christ). THEOLOGICAL: Christ, as the uniquely begotten Son, is the one who guards (τηρεῖ, tērei) the believer so that the evil one (ὁ πονηρός, ho ponēros — Satan) cannot "touch" (háptō, Strong's G680, meaning to fasten oneself to, to cling to with harmful intent) him. This is divine preservation, not sinless perfection.

verses.flow: We know that we are of God, and the whole world lies in the evil one.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The stark contrast — "we are of God" versus "the whole world lies in the evil one" — reflects a cosmic dualism of allegiance, not ontological dualism. The evil one (ὁ πονηρός) is the usurping ruler of the present world-system (cf. John 12:31, 2 Cor 4:4, Eph 2:2), a rebellious member of the spiritual realm whose dominion Christ has come to overthrow. GREEK: "Lies" (κεῖται, keitai) suggests a passive, helpless state — the world is laid down in the power of the evil one.

verses.flow: And we know that the Son of God has come, and has given to us understanding, that we may know the True One; and we are in the True One, in His Son Jesus Christ. This One is the true God and eternal life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "This One is the true God and eternal life" (οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἀληθινὸς θεὸς καὶ ζωὴ αἰώνιος) is one of the strongest Christological affirmations in the New Testament. The proximate referent of "this one" (houtos) is Jesus Christ, identifying Him directly as "the true God" (ho alēthinos theos). GREEK: "Understanding" (διάνοιαν, dianoian, Strong's G1271) refers to the faculty of intellectual and spiritual perception — the Son has granted a renewed mind capable of knowing the True One. TYPOLOGY: The "True One" (τὸν ἀληθινόν) echoes the covenant name theology of the Old Testament where YHWH alone is the true and living God over against all idols (cf. Jer 10:10), setting up the final exhortation in v.21.

verses.flow: Little children, guard yourselves from idols.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The abrupt closing — "guard yourselves from idols" (εἰδώλων, eidōlōn, Strong's G1497) — is not a non sequitur but the natural conclusion of the entire epistle. Any object of ultimate allegiance that is not the true God revealed in Jesus Christ is an idol. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In Second Temple Jewish thought, idols were understood as connected to rebellious spiritual beings (cf. Deut 32:17; Ps 106:37; 1 Cor 10:20). John's warning thus carries both theological and spiritual-warfare dimensions.
